title: Quick check: install failure, GPO or policy block

questions.prompt: Policy blocks and AV blocks both produce 'access denied' symptoms. Which diagnostic separates them?
choices: Reading the install logs and the system event log together.

choices.label: Checking the endpoint's hostname pattern.
Asking the customer which one is more likely.
Re-running the install and timing how fast it fails.

questions.explanation: Policy mechanisms fire identifiable events around the install timestamp. The event log names the mechanism; the install log alone can mislead.

questions.prompt: Where do AppLocker events fire in the Windows Event Log?

choices.label: Under `Microsoft-Windows-Defender`.
choices.rationale: That's where Defender fires; AV territory (lesson 14).

choices.label: Under `Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon`.
choices.rationale: Sysmon is its own channel and isn't tied to AppLocker.

choices.label: Under the Security log only.
choices.rationale: The Security log captures auditing, not AppLocker rule decisions specifically.

questions.prompt: Which event channel does WDAC (Windows Defender Application Control) fire under?

choices.label: `Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker`.
choices.rationale: That's AppLocker's channel, a different policy mechanism.

choices.label: The Application log.
choices.rationale: Too generic; WDAC has its own dedicated channel.

choices.label: `Microsoft-Windows-Firewall`.
choices.rationale: Wrong subsystem; firewalls don't enforce WDAC.

questions.prompt: Why is 'asking for a blanket allow-all rule for the Huntress installer' called out as wrong?

choices.label: Blanket allows make the install slower.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason; performance isn't the framing.

choices.label: Huntress refuses to install if a blanket rule is in place.
Blanket allows trigger Critical incidents.

questions.explanation: The runbook lists the specific objects (paths, certificates, hashes) to allow. Blanket allows expand the policy's attack surface unnecessarily. Specific allow rules are the contract; blanket allows weaken the policy the customer set up on purpose.

questions.prompt: You confirmed with the customer's IT that Bitdefender exclusions are in place. The install still fails with 'access denied.' What's the next diagnostic step?

choices.label: Re-confirm the Bitdefender exclusions and ask them to 'make really sure they're applied.'
choices.rationale: The exclusions are in place; the failure is something else. Re-checking is pressing the wrong button harder.

choices: Pull the Windows Event Log around the install timestamp and look for AppLocker / CodeIntegrity / GPO denial events.

choices.label: Reboot the endpoint and try again.
choices.rationale: You haven't diagnosed anything; rebooting changes nothing about the policy state.

questions.explanation: With AV ruled out, the symptom points at policy. The event log will name the mechanism.

questions.prompt: The event log shows AppLocker firing against the Huntress installer binary. The customer's IT confirms AppLocker is GPO-managed on this OU because executives have a tighter policy. What do you do?
choices: Ask the customer's IT to add the Huntress installer, agent binary.

choices.label: Ask the customer to remove this endpoint from the executive OU temporarily.
choices.rationale: You'd be asking the customer to weaken a policy they configured for a reason.

choices.label: Bypass AppLocker via PowerShell as a one-off install.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Running code to evade a security control. The next upgrade fails the same way.

questions.explanation: Signed certificate to the AppLocker allow-list at the GPO level, then push and wait for refresh. Identify the mechanism (AppLocker via GPO), use the right scope (GPO not local), and add the right allow-list contents per the runbook's canonical list.

questions.prompt: 'Applying the fix locally on the endpoint rather than at the policy mechanism' — why is this wrong?
choices: Survives until the next refresh, then evaporates.

choices.label: Local fixes corrupt the GPO database.
Huntress requires policy-level allow rules to register.
Local fixes only apply to administrator accounts.

questions.explanation: Push at the policy level. Same mirror as the AV-local-exclusions mistake from lesson 14.

questions.prompt: Which escalation surface does the lesson name for policy-blocked installs?
choices: The policy mechanism is one neither your MSP nor the customer's IT recognises.

choices.label: The endpoint is running a current supported Windows version.
The customer's IT speaks English.
The install succeeded and registered cleanly.
choices.rationale: Expected outcome, not an escalation trigger.

questions.explanation: (a third-party endpoint hardening product, an older legacy mechanism). If neither side can name it, escalate before guessing.

questions.prompt: Which verify check confirms a policy fix is durable?
choices: The policy allow-rule is in place at the policy-management level.

choices.label: The endpoint has been rebooted three times since the rule push.
Huntress support has confirmed the rule via ticket.
The customer's IT has emailed the rule contents to you.

questions.explanation: Not just locally on the endpoint. Local-only fixes evaporate at next refresh; the discipline is to push at the right management layer.

questions.prompt: AppLocker, WDAC, GPO, and Intune each have distinct event-log or MDM fingerprints; reading those is what tells you which policy mechanism fired.
questions.explanation: AppLocker under `Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker`; WDAC under `Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity`; local policy in System; Intune via MDM-reported reason codes.
